title: Pump Operations Pack 1
description: Premium Driver/Operator scenario pack with attack lines, FDC operations, relay pumping, drafting, water shuttle, master streams, ladder pipe, foam, and multi-discharge scenarios.

hoses: 5-inch LDH supply line from hydrant outlet to Engine 181 intake
200-foot 1¾-inch attack line from Engine 181 pump panel discharge to front door

problems.title: Total Flow Requirement
problems.question: What is the total flow requirement for the operation shown?
formulaBreakdown: Total flow = number of flowing attack lines × flow per line.
problems.instructorExplanation: The operation has one flowing attack line. The ChiefXD 185 line requires 185 GPM, so the total flow requirement is 185 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add hydrant supply flow separately. The supply line provides water to the engine; the flow requirement is based on the attack line being supplied.

problems.title: Pump Discharge Pressure
problems.question: Determine the required pump discharge pressure for the attack operation shown.
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Round FL to nearest 5 PSI: 105 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: The attack line friction loss rounds to 105 PSI. Adding the 50 PSI nozzle pressure gives a required pump discharge pressure of 155 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: A common mistake is stopping at friction loss only. Pump discharge pressure must include nozzle pressure after friction loss is calculated.

problems.title: Attack Line Friction Loss
problems.question: How much friction loss is present in the attack line?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
problems.instructorExplanation: Using the 1¾-inch hose coefficient and the listed flow, the attack line friction loss is 106.1 PSI, rounded to 105 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add nozzle pressure when the question asks only for friction loss. Friction loss is calculated before nozzle pressure is added.

problems.title: Nozzle Pressure
problems.question: What pressure is required at the nozzle?
problems.instructorExplanation: The scenario information panel identifies the nozzle package. The ChiefXD 185 operates at 50 PSI nozzle pressure.
problems.explainMistake: Do not confuse pump discharge pressure with nozzle pressure. Nozzle pressure is only the pressure required at the nozzle.

problems.title: Hydrant Pressure Drop
problems.question: The hydrant static pressure is 70 PSI and the residual pressure is 60 PSI while supplying the current operation. Based on the pressure drop, how many additional equivalent attack lines could be supplied?
formulaBreakdown: Pressure drop = static pressure − residual pressure.
Percent drop = pressure drop ÷ static pressure × 100.
Driver Operator hydrant rule: 10–15% drop supports 2 additional equivalent lines.
problems.instructorExplanation: The pressure drop is 14.3% of static pressure. Under the standard hydrant pressure-drop rule, that indicates the hydrant can supply two additional equivalent attack lines.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use the 10 PSI drop as a 10% drop. The percentage must be based on static pressure: 10 divided by 70 equals 14.3%.

problems.title: Second Identical Attack Line
problems.question: If a second identical attack line is deployed, what would the new total flow requirement be?
formulaBreakdown: New total flow = flow per attack line × number of identical attack lines.
problems.instructorExplanation: A second identical attack line doubles the flow demand. Two 185 GPM lines require a total of 370 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not double the pump pressure to find total flow. Flow is added by line; pressure is calculated separately.

problems.title: Extended Attack Line Pump Pressure
problems.question: If the attack line is extended by one additional 50-foot section, what would the new required pump discharge pressure be?
formulaBreakdown: Extended hose length = 200 ft + 50 ft = 250 ft.
FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Round FL to nearest 5 PSI: 135 PSI.
PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
problems.instructorExplanation: Adding 50 feet increases the friction loss. The new rounded friction loss is 135 PSI, and adding 50 PSI nozzle pressure gives a new pump discharge pressure of 185 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add a flat pressure amount for the extension. Recalculate friction loss using the new total hose length, then add nozzle pressure.

problems.title: Apparatus Positioning
problems.question: How far should Engine 181 be positioned from the structure for this type of residential fire attack?
formulaBreakdown: Engine 181 should be positioned at least 30 ft from the structure when practical.
problems.instructorExplanation: For this FirePumpSim residential attack scenario, Engine 181 should be positioned at least 30 ft from the structure when practical. This keeps the apparatus close enough for an efficient attack stretch while maintaining a safer setback from heat, smoke, falling debris, and collapse hazards.
problems.explainMistake: Do not position the engine directly against the structure. Leaving setback space protects the crew and apparatus and keeps the front of the building accessible for hose movement, ladders, and other operations.

details.label: Hose layout
details.value: 5-inch LDH supply line from hydrant outlet to Engine 181 intake; 200-foot 1¾-inch attack line from Engine 181 pump panel discharge to front door

hoses: Main line: 200 ft of 2.5-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to wye
Branch A: 150 ft of 1.75-inch hose from wye to front door nozzle
Branch B: 200 ft of 1.75-inch hose from wye to Alpha-side nozzle
nozzles: Branch A: ChiefXD 185 fog nozzle, 185 GPM @ 50 PSI
Branch B: ChiefXD 185 fog nozzle, 185 GPM @ 50 PSI

problems.title: Branch A Friction Loss
problems.question: What is the friction loss in Branch A?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Branch A uses 1.75-inch hose, so C = 15.5.
Branch A hose length = 150 ft = 1.5 hundreds.
problems.instructorExplanation: Branch A is calculated independently from the wye to the nozzle. Using 150 ft of 1.75-inch hose at 185 GPM gives 79.6 PSI, which rounds to 80 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not include the main line friction loss or the wye appliance loss when the question asks only for Branch A friction loss. Calculate Branch A by itself from the wye to the nozzle.

problems.title: Branch B Friction Loss
problems.question: What is the friction loss in Branch B?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Branch B uses 1.75-inch hose, so C = 15.5.
Branch B hose length = 200 ft = 2 hundreds.
problems.instructorExplanation: Branch B is the longer branch line. At 200 ft of 1.75-inch hose flowing 185 GPM, the friction loss is 106.1 PSI, which rounds to 105 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use the Branch A length for Branch B. Each branch must be calculated separately, and the longer branch will have more friction loss at the same flow.

problems.title: Main Line Friction Loss
problems.question: What is the friction loss in the main line supplying the wye?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Main line uses 2.5-inch hose, so C = 2.
Total main line flow = 185 GPM + 185 GPM = 370 GPM.
Main line hose length = 200 ft = 2 hundreds.
problems.instructorExplanation: The main line carries the combined flow of both branches. Since each branch flows 185 GPM, the main line flow is 370 GPM. Using 200 ft of 2.5-inch hose gives 54.8 PSI, rounded to 55 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate the main line using only one branch flow. The main line supplies the wye, so it carries the total flow of both Branch A and Branch B.

details.label: Hose layout
details.value: Supply: Hydrant to Engine 181 intake; Main line: 200 ft of 2.5-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to wye; Branch A: 150 ft of 1.75-inch hose from wye to front door nozzle; Branch B: 200 ft of 1.75-inch hose from wye to Alpha-side nozzle

details.label: Nozzle package
details.value: Branch A: ChiefXD 185 fog nozzle, 185 GPM @ 50 PSI; Branch B: ChiefXD 185 fog nozzle, 185 GPM @ 50 PSI

hoses: Supply line from hydrant to Engine 181 intake
Line A: 200 ft of 2.5-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to portable monitor
Line B: 200 ft of 2.5-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to portable monitor
nozzles: Portable monitor with 1 3/4-inch smooth bore tip
sceneElements.elevation: Level parking lot operation, 0 PSI elevation correction

problems.title: Master Stream Flow
problems.question: Given the portable monitor setup shown, determine the master stream flow.
formulaBreakdown: Smooth bore flow formula: GPM = 29.7 × d² × √NP.
problems.instructorExplanation: The portable monitor is using a smooth bore tip. Smooth bore flow is calculated using tip diameter and nozzle pressure. A 1 3/4-inch tip at 80 PSI produces approximately 815 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use handline fog nozzle flow values for this question. A portable monitor with a smooth bore tip uses the smooth bore flow formula.

problems.title: Line A Flow
problems.question: How much water is flowing through Line A while supplying the portable monitor?
formulaBreakdown: The monitor is supplied by two equal supply lines.
Line A flow = total monitor flow ÷ number of supply lines.
problems.instructorExplanation: The portable monitor flow is divided between two equal supply lines. Line A carries half of the total monitor flow.
problems.explainMistake: Do not place the full monitor flow in Line A. Because the monitor is supplied by two equal lines, each line carries one-half of the total flow.

problems.title: Pump Discharge Pressure
problems.question: Determine the required pump discharge pressure for the portable monitor operation shown.
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
Flow is split between two equal supply lines.
FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Line A and Line B use 2.5-inch hose, so C = 2.
Each supply line length = 200 ft = 2 hundreds.
Round FL to nearest 5 PSI: 65 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: For a portable monitor supplied by two equal lines, calculate the friction loss for one supply line using half of the total monitor flow. Since both lines are equal, either line gives the governing friction loss. Add nozzle pressure and appliance loss to get the required pump discharge pressure.
problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate friction loss using the full monitor flow through one hose. The total flow is split between Line A and Line B before reaching the portable monitor.

details.label: Hose layout
details.value: Supply line from hydrant to Engine 181 intake; Line A: 200 ft of 2.5-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to portable monitor; Line B: 200 ft of 2.5-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to portable monitor

details.label: Nozzle package
details.value: Portable monitor with 1 3/4-inch smooth bore tip; Master stream nozzle pressure: 80 PSI

details.label: Elevation
details.value: Level parking lot operation, 0 PSI elevation correction

scenarios.title: Mid-Rise Apartment Building Standpipe Operation

hoses: Supply line from hydrant to Engine 181 intake
100 ft of 2.5-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to FDC
150 ft of 2.5-inch attack line from standpipe outlet to nozzle
sceneElements.elevation: Fire attack operating on the 5th floor

problems.title: Standpipe System Pressure Change
problems.question: What pump pressure change is required for the standpipe system in this operation?
formulaBreakdown: FirePumpSim standard FDC / standpipe system loss applies.
problems.instructorExplanation: The standpipe system adds a standard 25 PSI system loss. This pressure is added to the pump discharge pressure along with friction loss, nozzle pressure, and elevation.
problems.explainMistake: Do not treat the standpipe system as zero loss. The FDC / standpipe system adds pressure demand before the attack line and nozzle are supplied.

problems.title: Elevation Pressure Change
problems.question: What pump pressure change is required due to elevation for the fifth-floor fire attack?
formulaBreakdown: Each floor above the 1st floor adds 10 ft of elevation.
problems.instructorExplanation: The fire attack is operating on the 5th floor. Using the FirePumpSim floor rule, that is 40 ft above grade, which requires 20 PSI of elevation pressure.
problems.explainMistake: Do not count the 1st floor as 10 ft of elevation. In this rule, the 1st floor is grade level, so the 5th floor is 40 ft above grade.

problems.title: Total Pump Discharge Pressure
problems.question: Determine the total pump discharge pressure required for the standpipe operation shown.
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
FDC supply hose FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
FDC supply hose FL = 14.0 PSI, rounded to 15 PSI.
Interior attack line FL = 21.1 PSI, rounded to 20 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: The pump must overcome the FDC supply hose friction loss, the interior attack line friction loss, nozzle pressure, elevation to the fire floor, and the standpipe system loss. Added together, the required pump discharge pressure is 130 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate only the attack line. A standpipe operation also requires the FDC supply hose, elevation, and standpipe system loss to be included in the final pump discharge pressure.

details.label: Hose layout
details.value: Supply line from hydrant to Engine 181 intake; 100 ft of 2.5-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to FDC; 150 ft of 2.5-inch attack line from standpipe outlet to nozzle

details.label: Elevation
details.value: Fire attack operating on the 5th floor
